verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In reference to Daniel my answer will be that I did not say that he was not a prophet; on the contrary, I confessed in the very beginning of the Preface that he was a prophet.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But I wished to show what was the opinion upheld by the Jews; and what were the arguments on which they relied for its proof.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I also told the reader that the version read in the Christian churches was not that of the Septuagint translators but that of Theodotion.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It is true, I said that the Septuagint version was in this book very different from the original, and that it was condemned by the right judgment of the churches of Christ; but the fault was not mine who only stated the fact, but that of those who read the version.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: We have four versions to choose from: those of Aquila, Symmachus, the Seventy, and Theodotion.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The churches choose to read Daniel in the version of Theodotion.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What sin have I committed in following the judgment of the churches?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But when I repeat what the Jews say against the Story of Susanna and the Hymn of the Three Children, and the fables of Bel and the Dragon, which are not contained in the Hebrew Bible, the man who makes this a charge against me proves himself to be a fool and a slanderer; for I explained not what I thought but what they commonly say against us.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I did not reply to their opinion in the Preface, because I was studying brevity, and feared that I should seem to be writing not a Preface but a book.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I said therefore, “As to which this is not the time to enter into discussion.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Otherwise from the fact that I stated that Porphyry had said many things against this prophet, and called, as witnesses of this, Methodius, Eusebius, and Apollinarius, who have replied to his folly in many thousand lines, it will be in his power to accuse me for not having written in my Preface against the books of Porphyry.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If there is any one who pays attention to silly things like this, I must tell him loudly and freely that no one is compelled to read what he does not want; that I wrote for those who asked me, not for those who would scorn me, for the grateful not the carping, for the earnest not the indifferent.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Still, I wonder that a man should read the version of Theodotion the heretic and judaizer, and should scorn that of a Christian, simple and sinful though he may be.
